title: The Authorization Code Flow
description:
## Logging In a Server-Side Web App

You have a traditional **server-side web application** (a confidential client). Which sequence correctly describes the **Authorization Code flow** it should use to authenticate a user with Keycloak?

options.text: The browser is redirected to Keycloak's authorization endpoint; the user authenticates; Keycloak redirects back with a short-lived **authorization code**; the app's backend **exchanges the code at the token endpoint** for the access/ID/refresh tokens.
The browser is redirected to Keycloak, which immediately returns the **access and ID tokens in the URL fragment** — no code exchange step.
The app collects the user's username and password directly and POSTs them to the token endpoint to get tokens.
The app sends its client secret to the userinfo endpoint and receives tokens for the logged-in user.

explanation: The **Authorization Code flow** redirects the browser to the **authorization endpoint**, the user authenticates at Keycloak, and Keycloak redirects back to the app's registered redirect URI with a **short-lived authorization code**. The app's backend then makes a **back-channel call to the token endpoint** to exchange that code (plus its credentials) for tokens. Keeping tokens off the front channel is what makes this flow secure. Option **b** describes the **implicit flow**, which is **deprecated** because tokens land in the URL where they can leak. Option **c** is the **Resource Owner Password Credentials** grant — discouraged because the app handles raw passwords and it bypasses MFA/SSO. Option **d** is not a real flow; the userinfo endpoint returns claims, not tokens.

hints.title: Two channels
hints.body: The code travels on the front channel (browser redirect); the token exchange happens on the back channel (server-to-server). That separation is the whole point.
